coredata.dc:title: N, F-doped graphene quantum dots effectively inhibit the fibrillization of amyloid-beta peptide (1–42)
coredata.dc:creator: Pan, Yatao
Hou, Guangjing
Wang, Xiaojuan
Ran, Xia
Liu, Pingan
Guo, Lijun
coredata.dc:description: Developing novel inhibitors for the fibrillization of amyloid peptides and understanding the relevant assembly processes are essential for biomedical and biomaterial sciences. Herein, we have synthesized and characterized the nitrogen and fluorine co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, F-GQDs), and investigated their inhibition of the fibrillization of Amyloid-beta peptide (1–42) (Aβ42) by monitoring the assembly morphologies in the Aβ42/N, F-GQDs systems. Our results indicate that the N, F-GQDs functionalized with multiple surface groups effectively inhibits the fibrillization of amyloid Aβ42. The strong adsorption of Aβ42 on N, F-GQDs significantly reduces the generation of β-sheet structures and the cytotoxicity of Aβ42/N, F-GQDs aggregates. Accordingly, a morphological evolution from the long fibrils of Aβ42 to the spherical particles and unique closed-loop structures of Aβ42/N, F-GQDs have been captured. A possible interpretation for the observed phenomena in the presence of N, F-GQDs has been proposed by combining the different inhibition effects of other GQDs on the fibrillization of Aβ42 and the results from various control experiments. Moreover, the inhibition activity of N, F-GQDs demonstrates good adaptability to resist the fluctuation of pH value and the interference of divalent metal ions, facilitating their practical applications in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Fluorine doping
Inhibitor
Fibrillization of amyloid
Morphology evolution

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Inhibition activity and evolution of assembly morphology
Adaptability to pH and divalent metal ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Development Project of Henan Province
Young Core Instructor Foundation of Henan Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project No. 22105063 ), the Young Core Instructor Foundation of Henan Province (Project No. 2021GGJS023 ), and the Open Project of the State Key Laboratory of Crop Stress Adaptation and Improvement, the Science and Technology Development Project of Henan Province ( 222102210217 ), the Key Scientific Research Project of Colleges and Universities in Henan Province (Project no. 23A510013 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of N/F co-doped GQDs. (a) Synthetic route of N, F-GQDs, (b) AFM image and (c) Height distribution histogram of N, F-GQDs.

Spectroscopic analysis N, F-GQDs. (a) FTIR spectra, (b) XPS full survey, (c) F 1s XPS spectrum, (d) deconvolution peaks of C 1s XPS spectra, (e) High resolution XPS of N 1s spectra, (f) Absorption and photoluminescence spectra. The inset is optical image of N, F-GQDs in solution under UV light.

Morphology evolution of Aβ42 aggregation induced by N, F-GQDs. (a) AFM image of Aβ42 aggregates from 20 μM of Aβ42 alone and after 7 days of incubation at 37 °C. (b) and (c) Representative AFM images of Aβ42/N, F-GQDs aggregates from mixing 20 μM of Aβ42 with different concentrations of N, F-GQDs (2.0 and 20 μg/mL) and after 7 days of incubation at 37 °C. (d) Schematically illustrating the formation of the ring-like structure of Aβ42/N, F-GQDs. (e) Height profile along the white line in (b). (f) Histogram of contour length of the ring-like structure.

(a) Circular dichroism spectra of Aβ42 incubated various concentrations of N, F-GQDs (0, 2, 10, and 20 μg/mL). The dotted curve is the CD spectrum of Aβ42 monomers without incubation. The measurements were conducted after 7 days of incubation at 37 °C. (b) The cytotoxicity toward SH-SY5Y cells of Aβ42 aggregates induced by N, F-GQDs with different concentrations and 2 days of incubation at 37 °C. Error bars represent the mean ± SD (n = 8).
Fluorescence images of Aβ42 aggregates with the presence of N, F-GQDs (a), PEG-GQDs(b), and DMF-GQDs (c) after 3 days of incubation at 37 °C. (d) Zeta potentials of three different kinds of GQDs.
AFM images of Aβ42 alone at pH = 4 (a) and pH = 9 (b), and Aβ42 with 20 μg/mL of N, F-GQDs at pH = 4 (c) and pH = 9 (d). All samples were obtained after 3 days of incubation in 1 × PBS buffer.

Fluorescence images of Aβ42 aggregates after 3 days of incubation at 10 μM of FeCl2 (a) and 10 μM of ZnCl2 (b); Fluorescence images of Aβ42/N, F-GQDs after 3 days of incubation with 100 μg/mL of N, F-GQDs at 10 μM of FeCl2 (c) and 10 μM of ZnCl2 (d).

head.ce:title: N, F-doped graphene quantum dots effectively inhibit the fibrillization of amyloid-beta peptide (1–42)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Electronics, Academy for Advanced Interdisciplinary Studies, State Key Laboratory of Crop Stress Adaptation and Improvement, Henan University, Kaifeng, 475004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Crop Stress Adaptation and Improvement

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Electronics, Academy for Advanced Interdisciplinary Studies, State Key Laboratory of Crop Stress Adaptation and Improvement, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Joint Research Laboratory of New Energy Materials and Devices of Henan Province, School of Physics and Electronics, Henan University, Kaifeng, 475004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Joint Research Laboratory of New Energy Materials and Devices of Henan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International Joint Research Laboratory of New Energy Materials and Devices of Henan Province, School of Physics and Electronics, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yatao Pan and Guangjing Hou contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing novel inhibitors for the fibrillization of amyloid peptides and understanding the relevant assembly processes are essential for biomedical and biomaterial sciences. Herein, we have synthesized and characterized the nitrogen and fluorine co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, F-GQDs), and investigated their inhibition of the fibrillization of Amyloid-beta peptide (1–42) (Aβ42) by monitoring the assembly morphologies in the Aβ42/N, F-GQDs systems. Our results indicate that the N, F-GQDs functionalized with multiple surface groups effectively inhibits the fibrillization of amyloid Aβ42. The strong adsorption of Aβ42 on N, F-GQDs significantly reduces the generation of β-sheet structures and the cytotoxicity of Aβ42/N, F-GQDs aggregates. Accordingly, a morphological evolution from the long fibrils of Aβ42 to the spherical particles and unique closed-loop structures of Aβ42/N, F-GQDs have been captured. A possible interpretation for the observed phenomena in the presence of N, F-GQDs has been proposed by combining the different inhibition effects of other GQDs on the fibrillization of Aβ42 and the results from various control experiments. Moreover, the inhibition activity of N, F-GQDs demonstrates good adaptability to resist the fluctuation of pH value and the interference of divalent metal ions, facilitating their practical applications in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The self-assembly of amyloid-beta peptides into fibrils and further aggregation into plaques are closely associated with the pathology of Alzheimer's disease (AD) [1]. In the past decades, many efforts have been dedicated to inhibiting the fibrillization of amyloid-beta peptides using artificial inhibitors as potential treatment strategies for amyloidosis, such as antibodies, peptides, small organic molecules, and inorganic nanoparticles [2–4]. However, pharmacological results indicate that the strategy and efficiency of inhibitors still need to be improved by developing novel inhibitors and further understanding relevant mechanisms of amyloid aggregation [5,6]. As one kind of inhibition candidate, carbon-based nanomaterials have demonstrated potential in biomedical applications, due to their tunable physiochemical properties, good biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity [7–9]. For example, graphene nanoparticles can effectively interfere with the monomeric interaction of proteins to form large complexes [10]. The electroactive graphene scaffolds can induce directional differentiation of neural stem cells into functional neurons [11]. Fullerene C60 and its derivatives demonstrate strong inhibition ability on the β-sheet formation of Aβ16-22 peptides [12], while graphene oxide shows a good ability to regulate the amyloidosis of Aβ33-42 in a size-dependent manner [13]. Additionally, hydroxyl-modified single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNT-OH) have been reported to dissociate mature Aβ42 fibrils via strong Van Der Waals interactions [14]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with adjustable properties and feasible surface functionalization, have demonstrated the ability to cross blood-brain barriers to facilitate their potential biomedical applications [15–17]. The properties of GQDs in reducing the self-assembly and inhibiting the aggregation of Aβ peptides have been reported [18]. GQDs prepared by the oxidation cutting method shows the potential application in detecting peptide monomers and fibrils based on photoinduced energy transfer [19], while polymerized o-phenylenediamine GQDs can interrupt protein assembly with light-responsive photodegradation ability [20]. Sun and coworkers synthesized multifunctional nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots for targeting Aβ aggregation and imaging amyloid plaques in vivo [21]. In addition to the inhibition effect on amyloid aggregation, Xiao and coworkers demonstrated the potential ability of GQDs to transport neuroprotective peptides into the central nervous system, resulting in an improved learning and memory capacity of a model mouse [22]. The interaction between the Aβ peptide and the surface groups of GQDs is closely associated with the inhibition efficiency, which is usually weak and vulnerable to surrounding environment. Heteroatom doping of GQDs has been proved an efficient way for tuning the electronic structures and surface chemical properties, by regulating the types and distribution of surface active sites [23]. In addition to the special pharmacological property, doping fluorine has exhibited an effective way to control the lipophilicity and basicity of GQDs surface, and thus the binding affinities to target peptides [24]. The introduction of fluorine can also affect the metabolic stability and second structure of proteins. It has been reported that F-doped GQDs can significantly promote the formation of α-helix-rich structure to prevent the aggregation of hIAPP (Human islet amyloid polypeptide) [25].

To further explore the inhibition properties of GQDs and understand the induced assembly/disassembly process of Aβ42, we have synthesized the nitrogen and fluorine co-doped GQDs (N, F-GQDs) with a hydrothermal method and investigated their inhibiting effects on the fibrillization of Aβ42 and subsequent morphological evolution. With the introduction of fluorine, the as-prepared N, F-GQDs are endowed with various functional surface groups and demonstrate an improved inhibition ability of fibrillization of Aβ42, compared with that of PEG-GQDs and DMF-GQDs with different surface groups. The adsorption of Aβ42 on N, F-GQDs effectively reduces the generation of β-sheet structures and the cytotoxicity of Aβ42/N, F-GQDs aggregates. Accordingly, a morphological evolution from the long fibrils of Aβ42 to the spherical particles and unique closed-loop structures of Aβ42/N, F-GQDs have been captured. The inhibition effect of N, F-GQDs on the fibrillization of Aβ42 also demonstrates good adaptability to resist the fluctuation of pH value and the interference of divalent metal ions, facilitating their potential applications.

2.1. Materials. All the chemical agents were commercially achieved and used without further purification. Human amyloid-beta peptide (1–42) labeled with Hilyte Fluor 488 and purity>95% (detected by High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC)) was purchased from Anaspec company (Fremont, CA, USA). 0.1 mg/mL of Aβ42 powder was solubilized in NH4·OH solution, sonicated for 3 min at ambient temperature, then dispensed into centrifuge tubes and kept at −80 °C for future use. Hydrofluoric acid (HF), hydrochloric acid, sulfuric acid, hydrogen peroxide (30%), ammonium hydroxide (NH4·OH, 28%), polyethylene glycol 4000 (PEG-4000), (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), anhydrous citric acid, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4 contained 0.1 M Na2HPO4,0.1 M NaH2PO4 and 0.15 M NaCl) and N, N′- dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Kalium permanganicum (KMnO4), iron dichloride (FeCl2), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), and sodium hydroxide powder were provided by local vendors.

N, F-GQDs. 1.0 g of anhydrous citric acid was dissolved in the mixture of 15 mL NH4·OH and 5 mL HF, and sonicated for 5 min. The resulting solution was put into a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. Then, the reaction product was cooled to room temperature in ambient conditions and obtained as a brown-black suspension, followed by the filtration with 0.22 μm microporous membrane to eliminate large particles. The resulting suspension was subjected to dialysis (molecular weight cut-off: 1000 Da) for 48 h. Finally, a brown powder of N, F-GQDs was obtained after a freeze-drying process.

PEG-modified graphene quantum dots (PEG-GQDs). 1.0 g PEG-4000 and 1.0 g anhydrous citric acid were put into a 10 mL glass bottle and heated to 180 °C using an IKA heating plate. Several minutes later, the mixture was melted with a change of color from light yellow to deep orange, indicating the formation of PEG-GQDs [26]. The product was dispersed in NaOH aqueous solution under ultrasonication, followed by filtration, dialysis, and drying processes as described previously. Finally, the yellow powder of PEG-GQDs was obtained for future use.

DMF-GQDs. Monolayer graphene oxide was synthesized by a modified solvothermal method [27,28]. Briefly, 2.0 g graphene micro sheets were added into 60 mL concentrated sulfuric acid and stirred intensely for 1 h at 25 °C. Then, 8.0 g KMnO4 was slowly introduced into the suspension within 30 min, followed by an incubation process at 25 °C for 12 h. The obtained dark green product was added into 1000 mL ultrapure water, accompanied by a slow injection of 25 mL H2O2 to eliminate excess KMnO4. The resulting sample was further washed with diluted hydrochloric acid five times by high-speed centrifugation and dried by heat-drying in a vacuum. Then, 500 mg of the obtained graphene oxide powder was added to 20 mL DMF solvent. The mixture was ultrasonicated for 15 min and transferred in a Teflon-lined tubular reactor, followed by heating at 200 °C for 12 h. The supernatant containing GQDs was collected after discarding the precipitates and cooled to room temperature. Finally, the resulting product was further treated by rotary evaporation to remove excess DMF and freeze drying to obtain the black powder of DMF-GQDs.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded by a Cary5000 UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence spectra were measured on an LS55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). XRD measurements were performed on a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer with the Cuκα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å, 40 kV, 40 mA). Sessile drop contact angles were obtained using an optical contact angle meter DSA25S (KRUSSADVANCE, Germany) with a water drop volume of 5 μL at room temperature. Dynamical light scattering (DLS) measurement was carried out on a particle-size analyzer SZ-100 (Horiba, Japan). The circular dichroism spectrum was recorded on a Chirascan 100 spectrometer (Applied Photophysics, UK). The needed solutions of various concentrations for different GQDs were prepared by dissolving a certain amount of relevant GQDs powder in phosphate buffer.

AFM Imaging. Chemically modified substrates for AFM imaging were prepared by depositing 20 μL of APTES (1%, v/v in ethanol) onto the freshly cleaved mica surface and incubating for more than 30 min. Then, the substrates were rinsed with deionized water and dried at 80 °C for 3 h. Morphology and size distribution measurements were performed under the tapping mode of AFM on an NT-MDT-Solver P47H-Pro (Russia) with NSG 11 tips (silicon cantilever). Sample preparation was carried out by a sequential process, dropping the sample solution onto a chemically modified mica surface, incubating for 10 min, rinsing with ultrapure water, and drying under N2 flow.

Fluorescence Imaging. A homebuilt single-molecule total internal reflection fluorescence microscope (TIRFM) (Ti-E2000, Nikon) was used to observe the morphology of Aβ42 aggregates and GQDs, equipped with an oil immersion objective (100 × , N.A. 1.49) and a CW 488 nm laser (Coherent, USA) as the excitation source. Coverslips were washed three times by successively sonicating in acetone, ethanol, and 1 M KOH for 30 min, then rinsed with water and dried under a flow of N2. The fluorescence signals were recorded by an EMCCD (Photometrics, USA) detector and analyzed by NIS-Elements AR software (Nikon, Japan).

Cell viability assay. SH-SY5Y cells were grown in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), 10% fetal bovine serum, and 1% penicillin-streptomycin in humidified (95% air, 5% CO2) atmosphere at 37 °C. The cells were plated in a 96-well plate and cultured overnight for cell attachment. Then, cells were incubated with N, F-GQDs, or peptide-containing media and treated for 48 h. After then, the culture medium was discarded, and 50 μL of MTT (1 mg/mL) in 1 × PBS was added into each cell-containing well, followed by incubation for four more hours. Finally, 100 μL formazan-dissolving DMSO was added into each well, and the viability of cells was conducted by measuring absorbance at 570 nm on Infinite M Plex (Tecan).

The N, F-GQDs were prepared by pyrolyzing citric acid to form a graphite-like structure under hydrothermal conditions using ammonium hydroxide and hydrofluoric acid as the N and F sources. The synthetic route is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 a. As shown in the AFM image (Fig. 1b) and the height distribution histogram (Fig. 1c), the as-prepared N, F-GQDs demonstrate a spherical shape, a narrow size distribution of 0.5–4.0 nm and an average height of 1.5 nm obtained by Gaussian fitting the histogram. The DLS histogram demonstrates a good consistency in size distribution with that of AFM imaging (Fig. S1). The XRD pattern exhibits a broad diffraction peak centered around 26° assigned to the (002) planes of graphite with an interlayer distance of 0.34 nm (Fig. S1). Similar to the report, replacing carbon atoms with N/F dopants or inserting N/F into the interlayer of graphene would change the inter-planar spacing and induce the lattice strain, resulting in a broad diffraction band [29,30].

To characterize the surface chemical groups of N, F-GQDs, we performed the FTIR and XPS spectral measurements and analysis. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the FTIR spectra display the stretching vibrational modes of O–H at 3422 cm−1, N–H at 745 cm−1, and 3163 cm−1 from aromatic amines, CO at 1667 cm−1, CC at 1527 cm−1, and C–N bond at 1403 cm−1, as well as the characteristic bond C–F at 1215 cm−1 [31,32]. These results suggest many nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of N, F-GQDs, and confirm that N and F atoms have been co-doped into GQDs during the carbonization process. The surface compositions and chemical states of N, F-GQDs were further characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS full spectrum and F 1s spectra are shown in Fig. 2b and c, respectively. We can observe the intense peaks of carbon (C 1s), oxygen (O 1s), nitrogen (N 1s), and fluorine (F 1s) from the surface of N, F-GQDs. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) can be decomposed into four superimposed peaks at 284.5, 286.1, 287.4, and 288.5 eV from CC, C–O/C–N, C–F, and CO, respectively. Similarly, the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) confirms the existence of pyrrolic C–N–C at 399.5 eV, graphitic C2–N–H at 400.5 eV, and amino N–C3 at 401.5 eV, respectively [33]. According to XPS elemental analysis, we determined the atomic components of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and fluorine to be 33.09%, 50.93%, 12.38%, and 3.6%, respectively. These results indicate that nitrogen and fluorine atoms have been successfully doped into the graphene structure, and the surface of N, F-GQDs has been functionalized during the synthesis as well. Combining the results in the literature, we can deduce many nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of N, F-GQDs, such as –NH2, –COOH, and –OH groups [34,35]. Accordingly, the N, F-GQDs are endowed a good water solubility to facilitate their applications in biosystems. Moreover, the photoluminescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra of N, F-GQDs in aqueous solution are presented in Fig. 2f. We can notice two characteristic absorption bands located around 245 nm and 345 nm, which can be ascribed to the π-π* transition of conjugated CC bonds in the carbonized core, and the n-π* transition of CO bonds on the surface of N, F-GQDs, respectively [29]. The N, F-GQDs exhibit a bright blue emission around 450 nm under 365 nm excitation and an excitation-dependent emissive feature (Fig. S2), facilitating their photoluminescence imaging observations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition activity and evolution of assembly morphology
ce:section.ce:para: The inhibition effect of N, F-GQDs on the fibrillization of Aβ42 and the structural evolution of N, F-GQDs/Aβ42 aggregates were investigated using AFM imaging. The representative aggregates for the morphology observation were obtained by mixing a given amount of Aβ42 with different concentrations of GQDs and incubating at 37 °C for 7 days. As shown in Fig. 3 a–c, the morphologies demonstrate a significant difference of Aβ42 aggregates in the absence and presence of N, F-GODs. Specifically, Aβ42 can form many interlaced fibrils with an average height of around 7.0 nm (Fig. 3a). More detailed morphologies of Aβ42 alone at different assembly stages are exhibited in Fig. S3, demonstrating a characteristic fibrillization process from small particle to short fibril and thread-like fibril. In sharp contrast, when the Aβ42 solution was mixed and incubated with 2 μg/mL of N, F-GQDs, the distinct triangle and annular structures can be observed in the AFM images (Fig. 3b). With increasing the concentration of N, F-GQDs to 20 μg/mL, the fibrillization of Aβ42 is inhibited completely. Thus, spherical nanoparticles instead of fibrils are observed in the aggregation morphologies (Fig. 3c). These observations suggest a strong inhibition effect of N, F-GQDs on the fibrillization Aβ42. For the formed closed-loop structure, the height profile and the contour lengths were measured from AFM images and shown in Fig. 3e and f. The distribution histogram from the collected contour lengths of more than 30 samples can be fitted with unimodal Gaussian function, giving an average length of about 1.0 μm and exhibiting a good homogeneity.

To further understand the inhibition process and the possible origin of the triangular rings, we performed the measurements of circular dichroism spectroscopy to investigate the changes in the secondary structure of Aβ42 induced by N, F-GQDs. According to the literature [36], forming a stacked β-sheet structure is the feature of Aβ42 fibrillization. From the resulting CD spectra under an identical concentration of Aβ42 (Fig. 4 a), we can notice that Aβ42 monomers show a typical CD spectrum consisting of negative CD signals around 215 nm of β-sheet and 200 nm mainly from random coils. After 7 days of incubation without the presence of N, F-GQDs, a broad and negative CD band around 215 nm dominates the spectrum, indicating the fibrillization of Aβ42 and the formation of many stacked β-sheet structures. With increasing the concentration of N, F-GQDs, the negative ellipticity value around 215 nm is progressively decreased, suggesting the decrease of β-sheet content in Aβ42 aggregates. These observations indicate that N, F-GQDs can effectively suppress the conformational transformation of Aβ42 to β-sheet-rich aggregates and thus inhibit the fibrillization of Aβ42. The inhibition effect of N, F-GQDs could also be reflected in the cytotoxicity of distinct aggregates and conformations of Aβ42. To prove this, we applied a 3-(4,5-Dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assays with SH-SY5Y cells to assess the cytotoxicity of the formed Aβ42 aggregates without/with the presence of N, F-GQDs. We first tested the cytotoxicity of N, F-GQDs with different concentrations. As depicted in Fig. S4, the cell viability over 93% is still maintained by changing the concentration of N, F-GQDs from 5 to 100 μg/mL, demonstrating good biocompatibility. Comparatively, the Aβ42 aggregates without N, F-GQDs show a high cytotoxicity and a low cell viability of 56% (Fig. 4b). However, the presence of N, F-GQDS significantly improves the cell viability of Aβ42 aggregates. The viability can reach over 90% when the same amount of Aβ42 is incubated with 20 μg/mL of N, F-GQDs. These observations indicate the conformational transition to the β-sheet is a critical factor in the cytotoxicity of Aβ42 fibrils. Therefore, we can conclude that the N, F-GQDs can effectively inhibit the fibrillization of Aβ42 and alleviate the cytotoxicity of Aβ42 aggregates, which are also conducive to their potential applications.

As shown above, different secondary structures and aggregation intermediates of Aβ42 have diverse toxicities, while the Aβ42 aggregates show intense cytotoxicity. It was reported that the interfacial electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions between Aβ40 and substrate could induce the conformational transformations of Aβ40 [37]. The increment of contact angle from 23° to 75° can also lead to a continuous delay in the fibrillization of human islet amyloid polypeptide [38]. The fibrillary aggregation could be decreased by increasing the contact angle. For the negatively charged mica, electrostatic attraction through charged amino acid residues could cause the epitaxial growth of peptide, while the hydrophobic interactions could result in a flat allocation of amyloid sheets. In order to exclude the contribution from surface chemical properties of mica substrate to the aggregation structures, and especially to prove the observed morphology of Aβ42/N, F-GQDs aggregate arises from the interplay between N, F-GQDs and Aβ42, we investigated the adsorption and aggregation of Aβ42 on the flat model surfaces with different physicochemical properties. The differences in aggregation behavior and morphologies of Aβ42 on mica surface with different hydrophobicity and electrostatics were compared. The surface modification of mica substrate with 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) can significantly change its negatively charged and hydrophilic properties to positively charged and hydrophobic features. As shown in Figs. S5a and S5b, the freshly cleaved mica surface with a negative charge is atomically flat. A continuous monolayer film is formed on the mica surface after the thermal treatment with APTES. However, their corresponding static water contact angles are changed from 16° on bare mica to 77° on the modified surface (Fig. S6), consistent with the previous report [38]. Interestingly, there is no significant difference in the morphology of Aβ42 aggregates on two different surfaces (Figs. S5c and 5d), indicating a negligible effect of substrate properties on the specific morphologies of Aβ42. Therefore, the observed unique morphologies of Aβ42 aggregates are induced by N, F-GQDs in stock solution.

According to previous studies, the chemical properties of GQDs surface play an important role when interacting with Aβ42 for their diverse affinities to peptides [39,40]. In general, the possible driving forces for disturbing the aggregation process of amyloids consist of hydrophobic, π-π stacking, electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interactions. For example, carbon dots coated with carboxyl groups exhibit an intense inhibitory effect on human insulin assembly by bonding to histidine residues on B-chain [41]. H-bonding interactions of trehalose with the backbone of an amyloid peptide are more intense than the inter-peptide H-bonds, leading to a suppressed self-aggregation of amyloids [42,43].

Moreover, to further explore the possible regulation mechanism of N, F-GQDs toward the assembly behavior of Aβ42, we synthesized PEG-GQDs and DMF-GQDs. According to the morphologies of Aβ42 aggregates induced by different GQDs (Fig. 5 ), we can infer that the surface modification of GQDs by PEG molecules will provide lots of –OH groups to facilitate the hydrogen bonding interaction with the backbone residues of Aβ42. The inhibition effect of PEG-GQDs on the fibrillization of Aβ42 is less pronounced than that of N, F-GQDs. In other words, the hydrogen-bonding interaction via –OH groups between PEG-GQDs and Aβ42 could not effectively prevent the conformational transition to the β-sheet of Aβ42. For the much weaker negatively-charged DMF-GQDs, more amide groups are attached to the graphene edges. Comparatively, a lot of longer and larger fibrils can be observed for the DMF-GQDs (Fig. 5c), suggesting an accelerative effect on the fibrillization of Aβ42. It was proposed that the adsorption of Aβ42 on DMF-GQDs is mainly driven through the hydrophobic and π-π stacking interactions, leading to a much weaker inhibiting effect on the fibrillization of Aβ42 [44]. Even though Aβ42 can be adsorbed on the basal surface of DMF-GQDs, the relevant interactions are far from effectively suppressing the self-assembly of Aβ42 or even promoting the conformation transformation of Aβ42 from α-helix to β-sheet. Moreover, the difference in surface property of these three GQDs is also implicated in their zeta potentials (Fig. 5d). We can notice that negatively charged N, F-GQDs show a similar potential value to that of PEG-GQDs and a different charged surface of DMF-GQDs. Previous studies show that the presence of negatively charged phospholipids can facilitate the interaction with Aβ peptides [45], and the negative zeta potential of gold nanoparticles is crucial to affect the inhibition of fibrillization of amyloids compared to the positively charged nanoparticles [46]. Without the inhibitors, the amyloid nucleation is mainly driven by hydrophobic interactions, while hydrogen bonding and electrostatic forces play a predominant role in controlling the subsequent elongation process [47]. These self-assembly processes of Aβ42 would be regulated by the negatively charged and surface functionalized N, F-GQDs by disrupting the balance among various driving forces.

Combining the aforementioned results and analysis, we can deduce a possible scenario of the interactions between N, F-GQDs and Aβ42, and the subsequent evolution of aggregation morphology. The electrostatic attraction between the negatively charged N, F-GQDs and positively charged residue of Aβ42 in an aqueous solution mainly drives them into contact with each other [48]. The electron-withdrawing property of fluoride atoms will enhance the acid nature of carboxylic groups and facilitate this electrostatic interaction. The hydrogen bonding interactions through the multiple bonding sites of different surface groups (e.g., –OH, –COOH, C–F···H–O) play the dominant role in the strong adsorption of Aβ42 on the surface of N, F-GQDs. Accordingly, the fibrillization and the conformational transition to β-sheet of Aβ42 are significantly suppressed. The effective competition between the adsorption on N, F-GQDs and the self-assembly of Aβ42 prevents the formation of long fibrils. The formed β-sheet-poor particles of Aβ42/N, F-GQDs could dramatically interfere with or direct the subsequent growth and structural arrangement of Aβ42. There would be distinct mechanistic properties of the formed intermediate and final aggregates in the presence of N, F-GQDs. Therefore, the interplay of various factors and forces results in the formation of closed-loop assembly structures (e.g., triangular, trapezoidal, or circular) of Aβ42/N, F-GQDs. For the case of high concentration of N, F-GQDs, most of Aβ42 are adsorbed on the surface of GQDs and the spherical morphologies are observed. Due to the complexity of the relevant assembly process, more evidence is needed to elucidate the morphological evolution and the interaction mechanism of the Aβ42-N, F-GQDs system in future work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adaptability to pH and divalent metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the environmental adaptability of N, F-GQDs in suppressing the fibrillization of Aβ42, we performed the AFM observations under different pH and metal ions conditions. As shown in Fig. 6 a and b, there is no significant difference in morphology of Aβ42 alone after 3 days of incubation at pH = 4 and pH = 9, although there are more fibrils observed in the alkaline environment of pH = 9. However, the inhibition effect of N, F-GQDs on the fibrillization of Aβ42 aggregation is still significant (Fig. 6c and d). Specifically, the spherical particles of Aβ42/N, F-GQDs are dominant in the acid environment of pH = 4 (Fig. 6c). Interestingly, the closed-loop structures are still observed at pH = 9 in the presence of 20 μg/mL N, F-GQDs, demonstrating a high similarity of morphology evolution to the observations in the neutral condition of pH = 7.4. This pH-dependent morphology suggests a different inhibition rate of N, F-GQDs, which is consistent with the previous statement that fluoride atoms enhance the acid nature of carboxylic groups and the related electrostatic interaction. We can conclude that N, F-GQDs have a good adaptability of resisting the pH fluctuation in suppressing the fibrillization of Aβ42.

On the other hand, people suffering from AD usually show an abnormally high level of transition metal ions (zinc, copper, and iron) in amyloid plaques and synaptic domains of the brain, suggesting these divalent metal ions affect the fibrillization of Aβ in neurons [20]. As mentioned above, the as-prepared N, F-GQDs have nitrogen-containing surface groups, which could react with the divalent metal ions through the proton-transfer process. To further evaluate the inhibition effect of N, F-GQDs on the fibrillization of Aβ42 and their feasibility for practical application, we performed the morphology observations in the presence of Zn2+ and Fe2+ ions using a single-molecule TIRFM. As shown in Fig. 7 a and b, the large network structures of Aβ42 fibrils are formed in the presence of Fe2+ ion, while the relatively short individual Aβ42 fibrils are observed in the existence of Zn2+ ion. In contrast, the introduction of N, F-GQDs (100 μg/mL) into the Aβ42 solution with the identical concentration of divalent metal ions leads to the vanishment of elongated fibrils, indicating the inhibition ability and substantial adaptability against metal ions (Fig. 7c and d). We speculate that the polyaromatic surface groups of N, F-GQDs chelate with the cationic ions (Fe2+, Zn2+) and regulate their electrostatic interaction with Aβ42. These observations suggest the N, F-GQDs can effectively inhibit the fibrillization of Aβ42 under various environments, which are conducive to understanding the assembly/disassembly processes of amyloid peptides and developing novel carbon-based inhibitors.

In summary, we have synthesized the N, F-doped GQDs using a hydrothermal method to inhibit the fibrillization of Aβ42 and investigated the morphology evolution of Aβ42-GQDs systems under various conditions. The co-doping of nitrogen and fluoride into GQDs significantly regulates the physicochemical properties of N, F-GQDs, endowed with multiple surface functional groups and active sites to interact with Aβ42. The morphological observations indicate the negatively charged N, F-GQDs have a strong ability to suppress the fibrillization of Aβ42. The adsorption of Aβ42 on N, F-GQDs effectively reduces the generation of β-sheet structures and cytotoxicity of Aβ42/N, F-GQDs aggregates instead of cytotoxic fibrils. Meanwhile, we have successfully captured the morphology evolution from Aβ42 fibrils to the spherical particles and specific closed-loop structures of Aβ42/N, F-GQDs. Combining the inhibition effect of PEG-GQDs and DMF-GQDs, and the results from various control experiments, we can infer that the unique surface properties of N, F-GQDs are responsible for the suppressed fibrillization of Aβ42. The enhanced electrostatic attraction by F-dopant facilitates the approaching of Aβ42 to N, F-GQDs. The hydrogen bonding interactions through multiple sites lead to the strong adsorption of Aβ42 peptides on N, F-GQDs, and significantly inhibit their conformational transitions to β-sheet structures, thus preventing the fibrillization of Aβ42 and Aβ42/N, F-GQDs. Moreover, the as-prepared N, F-GQDs also demonstrate good adaptability in resisting the fluctuation of pH value and the interference of divalent metal ions, facilitating their potential applications. Our findings are significant for developing carbon-based inhibitors against the fibrillization of amyloid peptides and a deep understanding of their relevant assembly/disassembly processes.

Yatao Pan: Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Guangjing Hou: Investigation, Formal analysis, Visualization. Xiaojuan Wang: Methodology, Validation. Xia Ran: Conceptualization, Visualization. Pingan Liu: Conceptualization, Resources, Validation. Lijun Guo: Project administration, Writing – original draft, preparation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project No. 22105063), the Young Core Instructor Foundation of Henan Province (Project No. 2021GGJS023), and the Open Project of the State Key Laboratory of Crop Stress Adaptation and Improvement, the Science and Technology Development Project of Henan Province (222102210217), the Key Scientific Research Project of Colleges and Universities in Henan Province (Project no. 23A510013).
